# scc — Scoped Cascade CSS

A transparent, no-build, no-magic CSS microframework. It styles semantic HTML
by leaning on the NATIVE primitives of the platform (@layer, @scope, custom
properties, color-mix(), light-dark()) instead of a tooling layer (Sass,
PostCSS, Tailwind JIT) or patch conventions (BEM).

It's the styling leg of a no-magic stack alongside Lumen (vanilla-JS UI,
Light DOM) and Agogo (server-rendered HTML in Go). The same principle across all
three layers: use the native platform, no compile step, everything readable and
auditable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## The thesis

CSS from 2024+ already has the primitives that made heavy tooling "necessary":

  - @layer          → deterministic cascade order. Goodbye specificity wars and
                      methodologies like ITCSS.
  - @scope          → NATIVE component isolation. Goodbye inventing unique names
                      (BEM) to prevent style leaks.
  - custom props    → design tokens at runtime. Goodbye Sass compiled variables.
  - native nesting  → nest without a preprocessor.
  - color-mix()     → states (hover, etc.) derived without defining 10 shades.
  - light-dark()    → light/dark theme without JS or classes.

scc is those primitives, organized with a minimal, consistent convention. It
adds no runtime, no dependencies, and no build step.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## The golden rule

  If a class or attribute in the HTML describes HOW SOMETHING LOOKS, it's wrong.
  If it describes WHAT IT IS or WHAT INTENT it has, it's right.

Right:   <button data-variant="primary">   (intent)
         <article class="card">             (what it is)
Wrong:   <article class="rounded shadow p-6 bg-white">  (appearance)

Consequence: presentation logic ALWAYS lives in the .css, never in the markup.
The HTML stays readable and describes meaning. This is real separation of
concerns, possible only now that @scope provides native isolation (the
utility-first argument that "separation is an illusion" only held while CSS had
no isolation).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## The six layers (cascade layers)

Declared once in scc.css, in this order (from lowest to highest priority):

  reset       → minimal, modern baseline (box-sizing, margins, fluid media).
  theme       → THE KNOBS. A few values everything is derived from. Your file.
  tokens      → full scale DERIVED from theme (calc + custom properties).
  base        → UNCLASSED elements (body, h1, a, code…). Semantic HTML looks good.
  components  → one file per component, each in its own @scope.
  utilities   → the highest and smallest layer: structure and a11y, NEVER appearance.

Because each file assigns itself to its layer, the order of the @import rules
doesn't affect the cascade. The order is fixed by the `@layer reset, theme, ...;`
declaration. Predictable and auditable: a style in a higher layer always wins,
with no specificity tricks or !important.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Anatomy of a component

A single pattern for all of them. The root marker is the most semantic thing
that unambiguously identifies the component — in order of preference:
  - the semantic element itself, if it maps 1:1 (button, dialog, table, kbd,
    progress, meter, input[type]…),
  - a native role, when the role IS the component ([role="tablist"]),
  - an intent attribute ([data-tooltip]),
  - element + class when the element is ambiguous (details.menu vs details.accordion),
  - ONE class on the root when there's no element for it (.card, .field, .badge).
Everything inside is styled by its element/role. Zero classes per element.

A note on ambiguity: <button> is unambiguous, so it's the marker bare. <nav> is
NOT (a site bar, a breadcrumb, a pagination are all <nav>), so the site bar takes
a class (.navbar). Use the element bare only when it maps 1:1 to the component.

    @layer components {
      @scope (.card) {
        :scope { /* the root */ }
        h2     { /* only h2 INSIDE .card */ }
        footer { /* only footer INSIDE .card */ }
      }
    }

Variants and states:
  - Variants  → intent attribute: [data-variant="primary"].
  - States    → native pseudo-classes: :hover, :focus, :invalid, :disabled.
Never additional style classes.

The :scope-override pattern. Some components contain elements that already carry
an scc base — the tabs are <button>s, menu/chip items are <button>/<a>. The base
(button.css, base.css) lives in the SAME layer (components/base), so layer order
won't decide; specificity does. To make the component's restyle win WITHOUT
!important, prefix the inner selector with :scope (it adds the scope root's
specificity):

    @scope ([role="tablist"]) {
      :scope [role="tab"] { /* wins over @scope(button){:scope} — (0,2,0) > (0,1,0) */ }
    }

This is the one place specificity matters inside scc; it's local and explicit,
not a specificity war. Used in tabs.css, menu.css and chip.css.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## File structure

  scc.css              Entry. Declares the layer order and imports everything.
  reset.css            @layer reset
  theme.css            @layer theme   ← THE KNOBS: edit/copy this for your site
  tokens.css           @layer tokens  ← derived from theme.css; no need to touch it
  base.css             @layer base
  animations.css       shared @keyframes (no layer; referenced internally by components)
  themes/
    sunset.css         Example of forking the contract (same markup, different look)
  components/          one file per component — see the Component catalog below
    _template.css      copy this to author a new component (not imported)
  utilities.css        @layer utilities  (.stack .cluster .center .visually-hidden)
  demo/index.html      No-build demonstration.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Usage

  <link rel="stylesheet" href="scc.css">

That's it. No npm, no compiling, no watcher. The files reach the browser as-is;
HTTP/2 makes serving them unbundled practical.

Re-theme: change the values in theme.css. Override tokens per section or per
component: redefine the custom property in a more specific scope (works because
custom properties cascade normally).

  <section style="--accent: #16a34a"> … </section>

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Adding a component (reference)

Full human walkthrough with a worked example and checklist: GUIDE.md. Starter to
copy: components/_template.css. The conventions in brief:

1. Marker — most semantic UNAMBIGUOUS option, in order:
     element (1:1, e.g. button/dialog/table/input[type]) > native role ([role="…"])
     > intent attribute ([data-…]) > element+class (details.menu) > one class (.card).
     Litmus: would styling the bare element catch unrelated markup? Then use a class
     (<button> is safe; <nav> is not → .navbar).
2. Scaffold — @layer components { @scope (<marker>) { … } }; :scope for the root,
   element/role selectors for internals. Never per-element classes.
3. Tokens only — var(--…) for color/space/type/radius/motion; no raw hex/px/ms.
4. Variants → [data-variant] (ideally swap one local custom property, see alert.css
   --tone). States → native pseudo-classes (:hover, :checked, [aria-current], [open]).
5. Inner <button>/<a> carry the scc base → override with a :scope-prefixed selector
   (the :scope-override pattern above). Used in tabs/menu/chip.
6. Responsiveness via @container (not @media); motion via tokens + @starting-style;
   @keyframes go in animations.css.
7. Register: @import in scc.css (more specific component imported later), then a
   demo section.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Component catalog

27 components. Marker = the selector that identifies the component. "Behavior"
means a small amount of JS (or your view layer) is needed; everything else is
pure CSS. Variants are [data-variant] unless noted; states are native pseudo-classes.

FORM & INPUT
  input        input(text-like) / textarea             Global control appearance.
                                                       Excludes checkbox/radio/range.
  select       select                                  Native select + custom caret (appearance:none).
  field        .field                                  Layout only: label+control+hint.
  choice       input[type=checkbox] / [type=radio]     Native, themed via accent-color.
  switch       input[role="switch"]                    Checkbox as a toggle.
  button       button, [data-button]                   Variants: primary, ghost.

CONTAINERS & DATA
  card         .card                                   Container query: rows when wide.
  table        table                                   Toggles: [data-zebra] [data-sticky];
                                                        cells [data-numeric].
  accordion    details.accordion                       name="" → exclusive, no JS.
  dialog       dialog                                  Native modal. Behavior: showModal().
                                                        Entrance via @starting-style.

NAVIGATION
  nav          .navbar                                 [data-brand], aria-current,
                                                        [data-nav-end].
  tabs         [role="tablist"] / [role="tabpanel"]    Behavior: toggle aria-selected/[hidden].
  menu         details.menu                            Dropdown via <details>. No light-dismiss.
  breadcrumb   .breadcrumb (on the list)               Wrap in <nav aria-label>. CSS separators.
  pagination   .pagination (on the list)               Wrap in <nav aria-label>. aria-current.

FEEDBACK & STATUS
  alert        .alert                                  Block callout. Variants via --tone.
  toast        .toasts (region) + .toast               Behavior: append/remove. @starting-style.
  badge        .badge                                  Inline pill. accent/success/warning/danger.
  chip         .chip                                   Dismissible tag (the × is behavior).
  tooltip      [data-tooltip]                          ::after + attr(). Hints only; see a11y note.
  progress     progress                                Vendor pseudo-elements.
  meter        meter                                   Bands colored from status tokens.
  skeleton     .skeleton                               Shimmer; paused under reduced-motion.
  spinner      .spinner                                Indeterminate loader; follows currentColor.

PRIMITIVES
  avatar       .avatar                                 Image or initials. Size: --avatar-size.
  kbd          kbd                                     Keycap. Nestable for combos.
  divider      .divider                                Rule with optional centered label.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Themes — the theme is the starting point

Motto: no two sites should look alike. A theme isn't a finished look but a panel
of knobs. theme.css defines a few inputs; tokens.css derives from them the
modular type scale (--ratio), the spacing rhythm (--space-base) and even the
gray ramp, which is TINTED with the brand hue (--neutral-hue/-chroma via oklch).
Motion is part of the identity too: a --dur scale (fast/default/slow) and the
easings (--ease, --ease-bounce) — swap them and the UI's movement changes
character. Changing two values reconfigures the character of the whole UI.

Creating your own site = editing theme.css, or copying it. tokens.css and the
components are left untouched.

TWO ORTHOGONAL AXES (don't mix them):

  data-mode   = MODE, lighting condition.  light · dark · absent = auto (OS)
  data-theme  = THEME, brand identity.     sunset · … · absent = default

They compose: <html data-mode="dark"> + <section data-theme="sunset"> = sunset dark.

AXIS 1 · MODE. light-dark() resolves the pair from the COMPUTED color-scheme. By
default it follows the OS. To force the mode, NO tokens are redefined: only
color-scheme is changed via [data-mode] (see theme.css):

    document.documentElement.dataset.mode = "dark";  // "light" | "dark" | remove = auto

AXIS 2 · THEME. Redefine the contract tokens under [data-theme="x"]. Each theme
uses light-dark() internally, so it works in BOTH modes. It can be global (on
:root) or scoped to a section (<section data-theme="sunset">) → several themes
coexist on the same page. Important: a themed scope RE-DECLARES the inherited
properties (color, font-family) so the theme cascades inward (see the
[data-theme] rule in theme.css). See themes/sunset.css: same markup, components
untouched, completely different look (serif, dramatic scale, bold borders, no
shadow).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Breakpoints — "no breakpoints"

Stance: prefer NOT to have breakpoints. Modern CSS handles most responsive cases
without a single @media:

  - Container queries (@container): the component adapts to ITS width, not the
    screen's. It fits @scope; see card.css (column to row based on its own width
    with @container + :has(img)).
  - Intrinsic layout: flex-wrap, grid repeat(auto-fit, minmax()), min/max/clamp.
    The demo reflows on its own via .cluster (flex-wrap), zero media queries.
  - Fluid typography/space with clamp() (e.g. --text-base as a clamp()).

@media is left only for PAGE layout (a sidebar that collapses, etc.).

Honesty: breakpoints CANNOT be custom properties — `@media (min-width: var(--bp))`
and `@container (min-width: var(--bp))` are not supported. Making it DRY would
require a preprocessor, forbidden by principle. That's why in scc breakpoints are
a documented CONVENTION (agreed values), not tokens. Said plainly instead of
pretending it's DRY.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Trade-offs and limits

Strengths:
  - Zero dependencies, zero runtime, zero build. Nothing that rots from tooling.
  - Deterministic cascade and auditable code: what you see is what applies.
  - Semantic, readable HTML; presentation isolated in CSS.
  - Fits directly with Light DOM (Lumen) and server-rendered HTML (Agogo).

Limits (taken on purpose):
  - Modern browsers only. @scope: Chrome 118+, Safari 17.4+, Firefox 128+.
    light-dark()/color-mix() in the same range. It's the same price you already
    pay for using Lumen; it adds no new constraints to the stack.
  - Not utility-first: for very specific appearance cases, the right place is a
    component, not a utility class.
  - No utility scales (.p-4, .text-lg): it's a decision, not a shortcoming.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Lineage

scc is to CSS what Lumen is to Backbone/Marionette: a modernization of proven
ideas (BEM, ITCSS, OOCSS — isolate, order the cascade, tokens) rebuilt on native
primitives, without the preprocessor or the patch conventions that the platform
has already made unnecessary.
